candidates.reasoning: The landscape features classic East African savanna with flat-topped acacia trees and reddish-brown soil. The road quality and marking style are highly characteristic of Kenyan regional highways.

The terrain, vegetation, and soil color are virtually identical to Kenya, as both share the same East African plateau ecosystem. The road infrastructure is consistent with Tanzanian regional standards.
The semi-arid landscape and acacia-dominated vegetation are present in the southern and eastern lowlands of Ethiopia, though the road markings are slightly more typical of the Kenyan/Tanzanian style.

evidence: Paved two-lane road with yellow shoulder lines

candidates.reasoning: The landscape is dominated by Vachellia (Acacia) species with the characteristic flat-topped canopy typical of the East African savannah. This specific density and growth habit are highly representative of the Kenyan highlands and plains.
The vegetation is virtually identical to that of Kenya, as both share the same East African savannah biome. The presence of these specific umbrella-shaped acacias is a primary marker for this region.
Similar acacia-dominated landscapes exist in the lower elevations of Ethiopia, though the soil color and tree density here are more characteristic of the Kenyan/Tanzanian plateau.
The road features a white dashed center line and yellow outer edge lines, which is a common standard in Botswana. The driving side is left-hand traffic, consistent with Southern African standards.
Namibia shares very similar road marking conventions with Botswana, including the use of yellow edge lines and left-hand traffic in rural areas.
South Africa utilizes left-hand traffic and similar road marking schemes (white center, yellow edges) on many of its regional roads.
Zimbabwe also follows left-hand traffic and similar marking standards, though the road quality and specific edge line usage can vary.

The image quality and slight blur patterns are consistent with Google's coverage in Senegal. The specific style of the makeshift fence/wall on the left using local organic materials is common in rural West African regions, particularly Senegal.
The camera generation and image clarity match some of the rural road coverage in Ghana, though the lack of specific utility poles makes this less certain than Senegal.
The general image quality and the presence of similar rural road infrastructure are seen in Kenya, although the specific fence construction on the left is more characteristic of West Africa.

evidence: Google Street View camera generation/image quality

country_result:
Country: Kenya
Coordinates: -1.286389, 36.817222
Reasoning: The landscape and botanics agents both provided high-confidence assessments for Kenya, citing the quintessential flat-topped acacia trees and reddish-brown soil of the East African savanna. The regulatory agent confirmed that Kenya uses left-hand traffic and frequently employs yellow outer edge lines on national and regional roads, which matches the visual evidence. Finally, the meta agent's analysis of the Gen 4 camera quality and rural construction style further supports Kenya over other candidates.
